summary: Matt Rocklin recounts his journey from NYC to Austin, explaining his location history over several years of remote work. After starting at NVIDIA, he realized how much he missed being around nature compared to NYC's urban density. He explored LA and Austin before choosing Austin for its unique combination of walkability, bike infrastructure, and quick access to nature. His nomadic business travel lifestyle was interrupted by COVID, leading him to shelter with his parents in Southern California before finally returning to Austin in February 2021, just in time for the devastating Texas winter storm.
key_points: Austin's rapid city-to-nature transition and excellent bike/scooter infrastructure made it ideal for someone who values both tech scene access and outdoor activities
Living with parents during COVID was a positive experience that deepened cross-generational relationships, despite North American cultural stigma
The nomadic remote work lifestyle of living out of a small backpack became untenable once COVID hit
Location-based pay creates strange incentives for remote workers, like officially starting in California for higher salary
Simple, large tasks that don't require much decision-making can be satisfying in contrast to knowledge work
money_quotes: NYC is a fantastic city, but not great for those who prefer nature to people.
You can bike to a high-tech business scene, or to a swimming hole in about ten minutes.

There is stigma in North American culture about living with your parents. People who do this are sometimes considered unsuccessful. As someone who, by some metrics at least, is seen as somewhat successful, I'll happily wave the 'live with your parents' flag. It's great.
Cross generational relationships are probably things that we should cultivate more in our society.
There is something very satisfying right now about a large-but-simple task that doesn't require much brain power or decision making to make progress.
This rapid fall-off of city-to-nature fit my personality well.
key_insight: The best place to live is one that matches your personality's needs for both professional community and personal renewal through nature.
